articles.title: A PDF that changes based on who is reading
articles.summary: A PDF that changes based on who is reading is a visual format that stores instructions for where to draw glyphs on a page. The spec supports Tagged PDF, a structure tree that marks headings, paragraphs, lists, and most domains use it like government accessibility mandates or enterprise publishing pipelines but most PDFs you actually encounter are untagged. LaTeX, Chrome's print-to-PDF, most export tools don't produce tags so what you get is coordinates and font sizes. Text extractors read the draw commands left to right top to bottom hoping for the best and this didn't matter when humans were the only readers but now most PDFs end up in an LLM where we upload them ask Claude to summarize them pipe through parsers and every single one of these tools is fighting the same problem reconstructing structure from a format that never carried it.

articles.title: Unicode composition for filenames (2008)
articles.summary: Unicode composition for filenames is a problem that arises when different operating systems handle file names differently, resulting in varying codes for the same characters. This can lead to inconsistencies and potential issues with file naming and identification. The main concern is that the OS may not always return the exact filename given to it, especially if it's in NFC (Normalization Form Decomposed) form, which can result in different codes being used to represent the same character.

articles.title: Orthodox C++ (2016)
articles.summary: Orthodox C++ is a minimal subset of C++ that improves upon the original language by avoiding unnecessary features, such as exceptions and RTTI, which can lead to complexity and runtime costs. This subset is designed to be more readable and maintainable than modern C++, with a focus on simplicity and usability. It also avoids using certain C++ features, including streams, STL allocations, and exception handling, which are often considered unnecessary or problematic in certain contexts.

articles.title: yserver: A modern X11 server written from scratch in Rust
articles.summary: A modern X11 server written from scratch in Rust is being developed by a team that aims to provide a practical solution for running real desktop environments, window managers, and applications on modern Linux systems. The project's goal is not to clone Xorg but to create a standalone DRM/KMS (Direct Rendering Manager/Kernel Module System) server that can run various desktops and applications without legacy baggage. The team has tested the server with several window managers including MATE, XFCE, and Cinnamon, and it supports extensions such as BIG-REQUESTS, Composite, DAMAGE, DPMS, DRI3, GLX, Generic Event Extension, MIT-SCREEN-SAVER, MIT-SHM, Present, RANDR, RENDER, SHAPE, SYNC, X-Resource, XFIXES, XInputExtension, and XKEYBOARD.

articles.title: Nobody ever gets credit for fixing problems that never happened (2001) [pdf]
articles.summary: Nobody ever gets credit for fixing problems that never happened is a 2001 article by Andrew Hunt and David Thomas, which highlights the tendency of developers to downplay or ignore bugs in their code. The authors argue that this phenomenon is not just about technical issues but also about the social dynamics within software development teams. They contend that developers often prioritize their own work over others' contributions, leading to a lack of recognition for fixing problems that were never there.
